volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 1.2.215 - Section 2.2.367)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 1.2.215
sections.original: ego de eórum verbis famigeratorum insciens
sections.translation: From the reports of these tale-bearers , in my ignorance I rushed forward to rebuke my guiltless friend . But if the authority was always required from the foundation , upon which they speak of anything they have heard , unless that clearly appeared , the matter ought to be to the peril and loss of the tale-bearer .

sections.label: Section 1.2.220
sections.original: hoc ita si fiat , publico fiat bono ,
sections.translation: If this were so , it would be for the public benefit . I would cause those to be but few , who now that which they do not know [*] , and I would make them have their silly chattering more restricted .

sections.label: Section 2.1.223
sections.original: Multás res simítu in meó corde vórso ,
sections.translation: I am revolving many things in my mind at once , and much uneasiness do I find in thinking upon them .

sections.label: Section 2.1.225
sections.original: egomét me coquo ét macero ét defetígo ,
sections.translation: I tease , and fret , and wear myself out ; a mind that enjoins a hard task [*] is now my master . But this thing is not clear to me , nor has it been enough studied by me , which pursuit of these two I should rather follow for myself ; which of the two I should think of the greater stability for passing my life therein :

sections.label: Section 2.1.230
sections.original: amórin med án rei opsequí potius pár sit ,
sections.translation: whether it were preferable for me to devote myself to love or to aggrandisement ; in which alternative there is more enjoyment of life in passing one ’ s days . On this point I am not fully satisfied . But this I think I ’ ll do , that I may weigh both the points together , I must be both judge and culprit in this trial :

sections.label: Section 2.1.235
sections.original: ita fáciam , ita placet ; omniúm primum
sections.translation: I ’ ll do so — I like it much . First of all , I will enlarge upon the pursuits of love , how they conduce to one ’ s welfare . Love never expects any but the willing man to throw himself in his toils ; these he seeks for , these he follows up , and craftily counsels against their interests . He is a fawning flatterer , a rapacious grappler [*] , a deceiver ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.240
sections.original: cuppés , avarus , élegans , despoliator ,
sections.translation: a sweet-tooth , a spoiler , a corrupter of men who court retirement , a pryer into secrets . For he that is in love , soon as ever he has been smitten with the kisses of the object that he loves , forthwith his substance vanishes out of doors and melts away . Give me this thing [*] , my honey , if you love me , if you possibly can .

sections.label: Section 2.1.245
sections.original: ibi ille cuculus : “ ocelle mi , fiat :
sections.translation: And then this gudgeon says : O apple of my eye , be it so : both that shall be given you , and still more , if you wish it to be given . Then does she strike while he is wavering [*] ; and now she begs for more . Not enough is this evil , unless there is still something more — what to eat , what to drink . A thing that creates [*] a further expense ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.250
sections.original: nóx datur : dúcitur fámilia tóta ,
sections.translation: the favour of a night is granted ; a whole family is then introduced for her — a wardrobe-woman [*] a perfume-keeper [*] , a cofferer , fan-bearers [*] , sandal-bearers [*] , singing-girls , casket-keepers [*] , messengers , news-carriers , so many wasters of his bread and substance . The lover himself , while to them he is complaisant , becomes a beggar .

sections.label: Section 2.1.255
sections.original: haec égo quom ago cúm meo animo ét recolo ,
sections.translation: When I revolve these things in my mind , and when I reflect how little one is valued when he is in need ; away with you , Love — I like you not — no converse do I hold with you . Although ’ tis sweet to feast and to carouse , Love still gives bitters enough to be distasteful .

sections.label: Section 2.1.260
sections.original: fugit fórum , fugitat suos cógnatos ,
sections.translation: He avoids the Courts [*] of justice , he drives away your relations , and drives yourself away from your own contemplation . Nor do men wish that he should be called their friend . In a thousand ways is Love to be held a stranger , to be kept at a distance , and to be wholly abstained from .

sections.label: Section 2.1.265
sections.original: nam qui ín amorem praecípitavit ,
sections.translation: For he who plunges into love , perishes more dreadfully than if he leapt from a rock . Away with you , Love , if you please ; keep your owns [*] property to yourself . Love , never be you a friend of mine ; some there are , however , whom , in their misery , you may keep miserable and wretched —

sections.label: Section 2.1.270
sections.original: quos tíbi obnoxios fecísti .
sections.translation: those whom you have easily rendered submissive to yourself . My fixed determination is to apply my mind to my advancement in life , although , in that , great labour is undergone by the mind . Good men wish these things for themselves , gain , credit , and honour , glory , and esteem ; these are the rewards of the upright .

sections.label: Section 2.1.275
sections.original: eo míhi magis lubet cum próbis potius
sections.translation: It delights me , then , the more , to live together with the upright rather than with the deceitful promulgators of lies .

sections.label: Section 2.2.276
sections.original: Quo illíc homo foras se pénetravit ex
sections.translation: Where has this man betaken himself out of doors from the house ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.277b
sections.original: Pater , adsum , imperá quidvis ,
sections.translation: I am here , father ; command me what you will , and I shall cause no delay to you , nor will I hide myself in any skulking-place out of your sight .

sections.label: Section 2.2.280
sections.original: Féceris pár tuis ceteris fáctis ,
sections.translation: You will be doing what is consonant to the rest of your conduct if you reverence your father . By your duty to me , my son , I wish you , for my sake , not to hold any converse with profligate men , either in the street or in the Forum . I know this age — what its manners are .

sections.label: Section 2.2.285
sections.original: malús bonum malum ésse volt , ut sít sui similis ;
sections.translation: The bad man wishes the good man to be bad , that he may be like himself . The wicked , the rapacious , the covetous , and the envious , disorder and confound the morals of the age : a crew gaping for gain , they hold the sacred thing as profane — the public advantage as the private emolument . At these things do I grieve , these are the matters that torment me . These things am I constantly repeating both day and night , that you may use due precaution against them .

sections.label: Section 2.2.290
sections.original: quod mánu non queunt tangére tantum fas hábent quo manus abstíneant ,
sections.translation: They only deem it right to keep their hands off that which they cannot touch with their hands ; as to the rest , seize it , carry it off , keep it , be off and go hide , that is the word with them . These things , when I behold them , draw tears from me , because I have survived to see such a race of men . Why have I not rather descended to the dead [*] ere this ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.295
sections.original: nam hi móres maiorúm laudant , eosdém lutitant quos cónlaudant .
sections.translation: For these men praise the manners of our ancestors , and defile those same persons whom they commend . With regard , then , to these pursuits , I enjoin you not to taint your disposition with them . Live after my fashion , and according to the ancient manners ; what I am prescribing to you , the same do you remember and practise . I have no patience with these fashionable manners , upsetting preconceived notions , with which good men are now disgracing themselves .

sections.label: Section 2.2.300
sections.original: haec tíbi si mea imperiá capesses , multá bona in pectore cónsident .
sections.translation: If you follow these my injunctions to you , many a good maxim will take root in your breast .

sections.label: Section 2.2.301
sections.original: Sémper ego usque ad hanc aetatem ab ineunte adulescentia
sections.translation: From my earliest youth , even up to this present age , I have always , father , paid all submission to the injunctions you have given . So far as my nature was concerned , I considered that I was free ; so far as your injunctions were concerned , I deemed it proper that my mind should pay all submission to you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.305
sections.original: Quí homo cum animo inde ab ineunte aetate depugnat suo ,
sections.translation: The man who is struggling with his inclination from his earliest age , whether he ought to prefer to be so , as his inclination thinks it proper that he should be , or whether , rather so as his parents and his relations wish him to be — if his inclination conquers that man , it is all over with him ; he is the slave of his inclination and not of himself . But if he conquers his inclination , he truly lives and shall be famed as a conqueror of conquerors .

sections.label: Section 2.2.310
sections.original: tu si animum vicisti potius quam animus te , est quod gaudeas .
sections.translation: If you have conquered your inclination rather than your inclination you , you have reason to rejoice . ’ Tis better by far that you should be such as you ought to be , than such as pleases your inclination . Those who conquer the inclination will ever be esteemed better men than those whom the inclination subdues .

sections.label: Section 2.2.313
sections.original: Istaec ego mi semper habui aetati integumentum meae ;
sections.translation: I have ever esteemed these maxims as the shield of my youthful age ; never to betake myself to any place where vice was the order of the day [*] ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.315
sections.original: neu noctu irem obambulatum neu suom adimerem alteri
sections.translation: never to go to stroll about at night , nor to take from another that which is his . I have taken all precautions , my father , that I might not cause you uneasiness ; I have ever kept your precepts in due preservation [*] by my own rule of conduct .

sections.label: Section 2.2.318
sections.original: Quid exprobras ? bene quod fecisti tibi fecisti , non mihi ;
sections.translation: And do you reproach me , because you have acted aright ? For yourself have you done so , not for me : my life , indeed , is nearly past [*] ; this matter principally concerns your own .

sections.label: Section 2.2.320
sections.original: is probus est quem paenitet quam probus sit et frugi bonae ;
sections.translation: Keep on overlaying [*] good deeds with other good deeds , that the rain may not come through . He is the upright man who is not content with it , however upright and however honest he may chance to be . He who readily gives satisfaction to himself , is not the upright man , nor is he really honest : he who thinks but meanly of himself , in him is there a tendency to well-doing .

sections.label: Section 2.2.324
sections.original: Ob eam rem haec , pater , autumavi , quia res quaedamst quam volo
sections.translation: For this reason , father , I have thought that since there is a certain thing that I wish for ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.325
sections.original: ego me abs te exorare .
sections.translation: I would request it of you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.325b
sections.original: Quid id est ? dare iam veniam gestio .
sections.translation: What is it ? I am already longing to give assent .

sections.label: Section 2.2.326
sections.original: Adulescenti hinc genere summo , amico atque aequali meo ,
sections.translation: A young man here , of noble family , my friend and years ’ mate , who has managed his own affairs but heedlessly and unthinkingly — I wish , father , to do him a service , if you are not unwilling .

sections.label: Section 2.2.328b
sections.original: Nempe dé tuo ?
sections.translation: From your own means , I suppose ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.329
sections.original: De meo : nam quod tuomst meumst , ómne meumst autem tuom .
sections.translation: From my own means — for what is yours is mine , and all mine is yours .

sections.label: Section 2.2.330
sections.original: Quid is ? egetne ?
sections.translation: What is he doing ? Is he in want ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.330b
sections.original: Eget .
sections.translation: He is in want .

sections.label: Section 2.2.330c
sections.original: Habuitne rem ?
sections.translation: Had he property ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.330d
sections.original: Habuit .
sections.translation: He had .

sections.label: Section 2.2.330e
sections.original: Quí eam perdidit ?
sections.translation: How did he lose it ? Was he connected with public business [*] , or with commercial matters ? Had he merchandise or wares to sell , when he lost his property ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.333
sections.original: Nihil istorum .
sections.translation: None of these .

sections.label: Section 2.2.333b
sections.original: Quid igitur ?
sections.translation: What then ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.333c
sections.original: Per comitatem edepol , pater ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , my father , by his good-nature . Besides , to indulge his tastes , he wasted some part of it in luxury .

sections.label: Section 2.2.335
sections.original: Edepol hominem praemandatum ferme familiariter ,
sections.translation: By my troth now ! a fellow spoken of boldly , and as on familiar terms ;— one , indeed , who has never dissipated his fortune by any good means , and is now in want . I cannot brook that , with qualities of that description , he should be your friend .

sections.label: Section 2.2.338
sections.original: Quia sine omni malitiast , tolerare eius égestatem volo .
sections.translation: ’ Tis because he is without any bad disposition that I wish to relieve his wants .

sections.label: Section 2.2.339
sections.original: De mendico male meretur qui ei dat quod edit aut bibat ;
sections.translation: He deserves ill of a beggar who gives him what to eat or to drink ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.340
sections.original: nam et illud quod dat perdit ét illi prodit vitam ad miseriam .
sections.translation: for he both loses that which he gives and prolongs for the other a life of misery . I do not say this because I am unwilling and would not readily do what you desire ; but when I apply these expressions to that same person , I am warning you beforehand , so to have compassion on others , that others may not have to pity you .

sections.label: Section 2.2.344
sections.original: Deserere illum et deiuvare in rebus advorsis pudet .
sections.translation: I am ashamed to desert him , and to deny him aid in his adversity .

sections.label: Section 2.2.345
sections.original: Pol pudere quam pigere praestat , totidem litteris .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , shame is preferable to repentance by just as many letters [*] as it consists of .

sections.label: Section 2.2.346
sections.original: Edepol , deum virtute dicam , pater , et maiorum et tua
sections.translation: In good sooth , father , by the care of the Gods , and of my forefathers , and your own , I may say that we possess much property , honestly obtained . If you do a service to a friend , it ought not to make you repent that you have done so ; it ought rather to cause you shame if you do not do it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.349
sections.original: De magnis divitiis si quid demas , plus fit an minus ?
sections.translation: If from great wealth you subtract something , does it become more or less ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.350
sections.original: Minus , pater ; sed civi immuni scin quid cantari solet ?
sections.translation: Less , father . But do you know what is wont to be repeated to the niggardly citizen [*] ? That which thou hast mayst thou not have , and mayst thou have that misfortune which thou hast not ; since thou canst neither endure it to be enjoyed by thyself nor by another .

sections.label: Section 2.2.353
sections.original: Scio equidem istuc ita solere fieri ; verum , gnate mi ,
sections.translation: I know , indeed , that so it usually is : but , my son , he is the truly niggardly man [*] that has nought with which to pay his dues .

sections.label: Section 2.2.355
sections.original: Deum virtute habemus et qui nosmet utamur , pater ,
sections.translation: By the care of the Gods , we have , father , both enough for us to enjoy ourselves , and with which to do kind offices to kind-hearted men .

sections.label: Section 2.2.357
sections.original: Non edepol tibi pernegare possum quicquam quod velis .
sections.translation: Troth , I am not able to refuse you anything that you ; ask of me . Whose poverty do you wish to relieve ? Speak out boldly to your father .

sections.label: Section 2.2.359
sections.original: Lesbonico huic adulescenti , Charmidai filio ,
sections.translation: That of this young man Lesbonicus , the son of Charmides , who lives there .

sections.label: Section 2.2.360b
sections.original: Quin comedit quod fuit , quod non fuit ?
sections.translation: Why , hasn ’ t he devoured both what he had , and what he had not [*] ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.361
sections.original: Ne opprobra , pater ; múlta eveniunt homini quae volt , quae nevolt .
sections.translation: Censure him not , my father : many things happen to a man which he likes , many , too , which he does not like .

sections.label: Section 2.2.362
sections.original: Mentire edepol , gnate , atque id nunc facis haud consuetudine .
sections.translation: Troth , you say falsely , son ; and you are doing so now not according to your usual wont . For the prudent man , i ’ faith , really frames his own fortunes for himself : many things , therefore , do not happen which he does not like , unless he is a bungling workman .

sections.label: Section 2.2.365
sections.original: Multa illi opera opust ficturae , qui se fictorem probum
sections.translation: Much labour is requisite for this workmanship in him who seeks to be a clever workman in fashioning his life — but he is still very young .

sections.label: Section 2.2.367
sections.original: Non aetate , verum ingenio apiscitur sapientia ;
sections.translation: Not by years but by disposition is wisdom acquired . Age is the relish of wisdom — wisdom is the nutriment of old age . However , come , say what you wish now to give him .
